subtopics.subtopic_title: STEPWISE EVALUATION OF NON-RESPONSE BY DAY FIVE
subtopics.category: MANAGEMENT OF PERSISTENT FEVER DURING APPROPRIATE THERAPY
subtopics.concept: Persistent fever beyond day five during appropriate therapy for typhoid and enteric fever requires a systematic stepwise evaluation rather than reflexive antibiotic switching, as defervescence in enteric fever is often gradual. In uncomplicated cases treated with an active agent, clinical improvement in activity and intake is typically observed within 48–72 hours, and fever resolves within 3–5 days, though some children may take up to seven days. Persistent fever alone does not immediately indicate treatment failure unless accompanied by worsening toxicity, new red flags, or suspected complications. For cases with fever persisting beyond five to seven days without clear clinical improvement, the initial step is to confirm the basics: ensure the correct diagnosis, verify accurate weight-based dosing, assess adherence to therapy, and confirm drug quality. If culture and susceptibility results are available, they should be reviewed, and a repeat blood culture is recommended before changing antibiotics, particularly if prior antibiotics were administered or the initial sampling volume was inadequate. If the child develops vomiting or is unable to drink, this constitutes disposition failure and necessitates inpatient care. Concurrently, clinicians should actively evaluate for alternative diagnoses and complications. Rechecking a complete blood count (CBC) and inflammatory markers, such as C-reactive protein (CRP), is essential if the clinical status changes. Falling hemoglobin or platelets may indicate bleeding, while rising neutrophils or focal signs may suggest secondary bacterial infection. Focused imaging should be performed in cases of abdominal pain, distension, or tenderness, and pneumonia should be considered if cough or respiratory distress develops. For children on oral therapy, escalation to inpatient intravenous ceftriaxone or cefotaxime with supportive care is recommended while awaiting results. This systematic approach ensures rational therapy adjustments and optimizes outcomes in paediatric patients with persistent fever during typhoid and enteric fever treatment.

case_studies.title: Stepwise Evaluation of Persistent Fever in Typhoid
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural area in India presents on day five of treatment for culture-confirmed typhoid fever. He was started on cefixime, dosed appropriately for his weight, based on local susceptibility patterns. The parents report mild improvement in appetite but persistent fever, peaking at 102°F daily. He has no vomiting, abdominal pain, or breathing difficulties, and his oral intake has been fair. On examination, he appears tired but is alert, with a temperature of 101.8°F. His abdomen is soft, non-tender, and without distension. There are no rashes, hepatosplenomegaly, or signs of dehydration. A review of medication adherence reveals that the family has given all prescribed doses. His initial blood culture grew Salmonella Typhi sensitive to cefixime, but no repeat cultures have been done. CBC shows a hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, WBC 6,300/mm³, and platelets 210,000/mm³. CRP was not repeated. The family is anxious about the persistent fever and requests further guidance.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate persistent fever beyond day five of appropriate therapy for typhoid fever using a systematic approach, ensuring diagnosis accuracy, adherence, and absence of complications before considering treatment failure.

case_studies.title: Stepwise Evaluation of Persistent Fever in Typhoid
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy from a rural village in India is brought to the clinic on day five of treatment for culture-confirmed typhoid fever. He had been started on oral cefixime at an appropriate weight-based dose after a positive blood culture with Salmonella Typhi sensitive to cefixime. Initially, his fever was high-grade, and he had associated fatigue, poor appetite, and abdominal pain. Although his appetite has mildly improved, the parents report that he continues to have daily fevers up to 101°F and appears more irritable. They deny vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal distension, but note that he has been eating and drinking less over the past two days. On examination, his temperature is 100.8°F, and he is mildly dehydrated with a dry tongue and delayed capillary refill of 3 seconds. There is no abdominal tenderness, hepatosplenomegaly, or respiratory distress. His weight is unchanged since the start of treatment. The family confirms adherence to the prescribed antibiotic regimen, and the medication was purchased from a reputable pharmacy. No repeat blood testing has been done since treatment initiation.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate persistent fever beyond day five of treatment for typhoid fever using a systematic approach, including adherence, dosing, drug quality, and consideration of complications or alternative diagnoses.

subtopics.subtopic_title: STEPWISE ESCALATION FOR SUSPECTED CEPHALOSPORIN OR AZITHROMYCIN RESISTANCE
subtopics.category: MANAGEMENT OF PERSISTENT FEVER DURING APPROPRIATE THERAPY
subtopics.concept: For paediatric patients with suspected cephalosporin or azithromycin resistance during the management of typhoid or enteric fever, stepwise escalation is guided by clinical deterioration or lack of improvement rather than fever alone, as most children defervesce within 3–5 days of effective therapy. Escalation should be considered probable treatment failure if, by day 5–7 of appropriately dosed therapy, the child remains toxic, develops organ dysfunction, or is unable to tolerate oral medications. Before modifying the antibiotic regimen, it is essential to confirm weight-based dosing accuracy, assess for missed doses, review prior antibiotic use that may have reduced culture yield, and evaluate for complications such as abdominal distension, guarding, bleeding, or encephalopathy, as well as alternative diagnoses like malaria, dengue, or scrub typhus. Repeat blood cultures in non-responders should be performed when feasible to enable susceptibility-guided therapy, which minimizes unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use and supports antimicrobial resistance (AMR) stewardship. The escalation process depends on the initial therapy. If the child was started on oral therapy (e.g., cefixime or azithromycin) and shows no improvement, the next step is to switch to a parenteral third-generation cephalosporin such as intravenous (IV) ceftriaxone, followed by reassessment within 48 hours. For patients already on IV ceftriaxone, azithromycin should be added or substituted if cephalosporin non-susceptibility is suspected based on local epidemiology, laboratory reports, or elevated minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC). Conversely, if azithromycin was the first-line therapy and the response is inadequate, switching to IV ceftriaxone is recommended rather than persisting with oral monotherapy. Dual therapy should be avoided unless cultures and clinical findings indicate resistance, as unnecessary combination therapy accelerates resistance development. In cases of confirmed or strongly suspected extensively drug-resistant (XDR) infection, shock, encephalopathy, or proven cephalosporin resistance, escalation to a carbapenem such as IV meropenem is warranted. Once susceptibility results are available, therapy should be de-escalated to the narrowest effective agent, ensuring an adequate treatment duration to prevent relapse. Discharge and review triggers were not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CARBAPENEM-BASED SALVAGE THERAPY FOR SUSPECTED XDR ENTERIC FEVER
subtopics.category: MANAGEMENT OF PERSISTENT FEVER DURING APPROPRIATE THERAPY
subtopics.concept: Carbapenem-based salvage therapy is recommended for suspected extensively drug-resistant (XDR) enteric fever in paediatric patients when there is documented ceftriaxone resistance, epidemiologic exposure to an XDR outbreak (including cross-border exposure in South Asia), or clinical failure with persistent toxicity despite appropriately dosed third-generation cephalosporins. Continuing the same cephalosporin or cycling multiple oral agents in such cases increases the risk of complications such as perforation, sepsis, and onward transmission. The preferred first-line salvage regimen is meropenem, administered intravenously in a hospital setting under close monitoring. The typical paediatric dosing is 20 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours, with higher doses of 40 mg/kg per dose reserved for cases of suspected or confirmed central nervous system (CNS) involvement. Azithromycin may be added to the regimen when susceptibility is likely or confirmed, as it retains activity even when cephalosporins fail and can help reduce bacterial burden while awaiting definitive susceptibility results. The total duration of therapy is individualized based on severity and source control needs, but 10–14 days of effective treatment is commonly required for severe or XDR disease, starting from the initiation of the active regimen. Once susceptibility results are available and clinical improvement is evident, therapy should be de-escalated to the narrowest effective agent. Oral step-down therapy may be considered only if specific criteria are met, including hemodynamic stability, the ability to tolerate oral intake, an improving abdominal examination, and a minimum of 48 hours afebrile. Early consultation with an infectious diseases specialist is strongly advised for cases of XDR disease or treatment failure to optimize management. Monitoring during carbapenem-based therapy should focus on clinical response, adverse effects, and laboratory parameters, although specific adverse effects and monitoring protocols are not specified in the source.

case_studies.title: Carbapenem-Based Salvage Therapy for Suspected XDR Enteric Fever in a Paediatric Patient
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural district in northern India presents to the emergency department with persistent high-grade fever for 12 days, associated with abdominal pain, malaise, and anorexia. His parents report a history of travel to a neighboring country 3 weeks ago, where an outbreak of drug-resistant enteric fever was reported. He was initially treated at a local clinic with oral cefixime for 5 days, followed by intravenous ceftriaxone for 7 days, but his fever and abdominal discomfort have worsened. On examination, he is febrile (39.7°C), tachycardic (HR 120/min), and appears lethargic. His abdomen is mildly distended with tenderness in the right lower quadrant but no rebound tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal leukopenia (WBC 3,800/µL) and elevated transaminases (AST 120 IU/L, ALT 95 IU/L). Blood cultures are pending. The treating physician suspects extensively drug-resistant enteric fever due to clinical failure on standard therapy and recent exposure to an endemic area.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate the need for hospital admission and initiation of carbapenem-based salvage therapy in a paediatric patient with suspected XDR enteric fever, focusing on triage thresholds and risk of complications.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL CRITERIA FOR INTRAVENOUS TO ORAL SWITCH
subtopics.category: CRITERIA FOR ANTIBIOTIC DE-ESCALATION AND ORAL STEP-DOWN
subtopics.concept: The clinical criteria for switching from intravenous (IV) to oral antibiotics in paediatric patients with typhoid and enteric fever are centred on achieving clinical stability and ensuring the child can tolerate oral therapy. Transition from parenteral to oral antibiotics should only occur when all the following conditions are met: the child is hemodynamically stable without escalating fluid or oxygen requirements, the abdominal examination shows improvement with no evidence of perforation or uncontrolled bleeding, the child can tolerate and absorb oral medications, and there has been a clear clinical response with at least 48 hours of defervescence. In cases where ileus, persistent vomiting, or worsening abdominal signs are present, IV therapy should be continued, and further investigations should be undertaken to avoid premature step-down. For uncomplicated cases admitted to the hospital, a minimum IV antibiotic course of 3–5 days is generally sufficient if the child responds promptly, while severe disease, delayed presentations, or suspected extensively drug-resistant (XDR) infections may necessitate longer IV therapy, typically 7–10 days. Laboratory trends, such as stabilizing hemoglobin and platelets and improving leukocyte counts, along with inflammatory markers like CRP, can support the decision to transition to oral therapy, although laboratory normalization is not mandatory if the child is clinically stable. The choice of oral step-down antibiotics should be guided by susceptibility results when available. For susceptible isolates, narrower-spectrum agents like amoxicillin or co-trimoxazole may be used, while azithromycin or cefixime is preferred for resistant cases. It is essential to ensure the total planned antibiotic duration is completed, which is commonly 10–14 days for cephalosporin-based regimens and at least 7 days for azithromycin-based regimens. Discharge planning is integral to the de-escalation process and includes providing written dosing instructions and arranging follow-up within 48–72 hours to reassess the child’s oral intake, fever pattern, and abdominal symptoms. This structured approach ensures safe and effective transition from IV to oral therapy in paediatric typhoid and enteric fever cases.

case_studies.title: Clinical Criteria for IV to Oral Switch in Typhoid Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old girl from a semi-urban area in India was admitted 5 days ago with high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and vomiting for 6 days. On admission, she was febrile (39.5°C), lethargic, and had mild abdominal distension with tenderness in the right lower quadrant. Her blood culture was positive for Salmonella Typhi, and she was started on IV ceftriaxone. Over the past 48 hours, her fever has subsided, and she is now afebrile. She is alert, tolerating small sips of oral fluids without vomiting, and her abdominal tenderness has resolved. Repeat abdominal examination shows no signs of guarding or rebound tenderness. She has stable vital signs, with no tachycardia or hypotension, and her urine output is adequate. The nursing staff reports she has been asking for food. Her CRP has decreased compared to admission, and her hemoglobin and platelet counts remain stable. The parents are eager to take her home soon.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify clinical criteria for transitioning a paediatric patient with typhoid fever from IV to oral antibiotic therapy.

subtopics.subtopic_title: WEIGHT-BASED DOSING WITH MAXIMUM DAILY DOSES
subtopics.category: WEIGHT-BASED ANTIBIOTIC DOSING AND DURATION TARGETS IN CHILDREN
subtopics.concept: In the management of typhoid and enteric fever in children, weight-based antibiotic dosing with defined maximum daily doses is critical to ensure efficacy and minimize errors, particularly in older children and during transitions of care. Both the calculated dose and the maximum daily dose should be documented to prevent under-dosing. Doses should be rechecked after any significant weight changes during hospitalization and when transitioning between intravenous (IV) and oral formulations, especially at discharge. Typical pediatric regimens, adjusted for renal or hepatic impairment and guided by local protocols, include azithromycin at 20 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) once daily for 7 days; cefixime at 20 mg/kg/day (maximum 800 mg/day) divided twice daily for 10–14 days; ceftriaxone at 75–100 mg/kg/day (maximum 2 g/day) given once daily via IV for 10–14 days; and cefotaxime at 150–200 mg/kg/day divided every 6–8 hours via IV for 10–14 days. Amoxicillin at 75–100 mg/kg/day divided three times daily for 14 days is used when susceptibility confirms activity, while co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim component 8–10 mg/kg/day) divided twice daily for 14 days and chloramphenicol at 50–75 mg/kg/day divided four times daily for 14 days are options when susceptibility is confirmed, with chloramphenicol requiring hematologic toxicity monitoring. For extensively drug-resistant (XDR) infections, meropenem at 20 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours via IV is recommended. The duration of therapy depends on the drug, severity of illness, and clinical response, but cephalosporin courses should not be shortened below 10 days in children with prolonged illness or complications due to the increased risk of relapse. Routine use of fluoroquinolones is discouraged in children in India because of high non-susceptibility rates and antimicrobial stewardship concerns; their use should be reserved for specialist-guided cases with proven susceptibility and clear benefit. These principles ensure optimal therapeutic outcomes while minimizing risks of resistance and toxicity.

case_studies.title: Weight-Based Dosing in Pediatric Typhoid Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old girl from a rural area in India presents to the emergency department with a 10-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and fatigue. Her parents report she has had poor oral intake, weight loss, and intermittent vomiting over the past week. She was initially treated at a local clinic with oral antibiotics but has shown no improvement. On examination, she is febrile at 39.8°C, tachycardic, and appears lethargic. She has mild abdominal distension with tenderness over the right lower quadrant but no guarding or rebound tenderness. Her weight is 25 kg. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL and a WBC count of 4,500/mm³. Blood cultures are pending. The treating physician considers admission for IV antibiotic therapy and reviews the weight-based dosing for ceftriaxone, ensuring the calculated dose does not exceed the maximum daily limit of 2 g/day. Transition to oral therapy is planned once clinical improvement is observed.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of weight-based antibiotic dosing with maximum daily dose limits in pediatric typhoid fever, including adjustments during transitions of care.

case_studies.title: Weight-Based Dosing in a Child with Persistent Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy weighing 28 kg presents to a rural clinic in India with a 10-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and fatigue. The fever has been continuous, peaking at 39.5°C, and is unresponsive to paracetamol. His parents report reduced appetite and two episodes of vomiting. On examination, he is febrile, appears lethargic, and has mild abdominal distension with tenderness in the right lower quadrant. No rash or hepatosplenomegaly is noted. Vital signs reveal tachycardia (HR 120/min) and borderline hypotension (BP 90/60 mmHg). A rapid malaria test is negative, and the child’s complete blood count shows leukopenia (WBC 3,800/mm³) and mild anemia (Hb 10.2 g/dL). The physician suspects enteric fever and initiates IV ceftriaxone. During the hospital stay, the child’s weight increases to 30 kg, prompting a re-evaluation of the antibiotic dose before transitioning to oral therapy at discharge.
case_studies.learning_objective: Emphasize the importance of calculating weight-based antibiotic doses and adhering to maximum daily limits during hospitalization and at discharge, particularly following weight changes or transitions between IV and oral formulations.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC MODIFICATIONS IN NEONATES AND YOUNG INFANTS
subtopics.category: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC MODIFICATIONS IN HIGH-RISK PEDIATRIC GROUPS
subtopics.concept: Enteric fever is rare in neonates and young infants but, when present, often manifests as undifferentiated sepsis rather than the classic stepwise fever seen in older children. Clinical features may include poor feeding, lethargy, irritability, diarrhea, abdominal distension, or temperature instability. Rapid progression is possible, and diagnostic tests can become less reliable following prior antibiotic use, necessitating a low threshold for hospital admission and initiation of parenteral therapy. Management should proceed under the framework of “sepsis with possible enteric fever.” Blood cultures must be obtained prior to starting antibiotics, and lumbar puncture should be considered in cases with neurological signs, persistent lethargy, bulging fontanelle, or seizures to exclude meningitis or other invasive infections. Empiric antibiotic regimens should address pathogens commonly associated with neonatal or young-infant sepsis while also targeting typhoidal Salmonella, guided by local sepsis protocols. Third-generation cephalosporins are frequently employed; however, ceftriaxone should be avoided in neonates due to risks of bilirubin displacement and interactions with calcium-containing infusions, with cefotaxime generally preferred. Intravenous therapy is recommended initially, transitioning to oral antibiotics only after clear clinical improvement and reliable feeding are established. Close monitoring is essential for dehydration, hypoglycemia, electrolyte imbalances, and evolving abdominal signs, as complications such as perforation and shock may present subtly. Early escalation to a high-dependency unit or NICU is warranted in cases of apnea, shock, encephalopathy, or increasing respiratory support requirements. Given the altered pharmacokinetics in neonates and young infants, antibiotic dosing must be verified against age-specific guidelines and renal function, with reassessment required following weight changes. Not specified in the source are the sensitivity and specificity of diagnostic tests, sampling pitfalls, or detailed interpretation dos and don’ts.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MANAGEMENT IN MALNUTRITION AND IMMUNOCOMPROMISED STATES
subtopics.category: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC MODIFICATIONS IN HIGH-RISK PEDIATRIC GROUPS
subtopics.concept: In the management of typhoid and enteric fever in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) or immunocompromised states, including those with underlying immunodeficiency, malignancy on chemotherapy, or chronic diseases, specific diagnostic and therapeutic modifications are essential. These children are at a higher risk of severe bacteremia, atypical presentations, and delayed clinical response. Enteric fever in such cases often coexists with other infections, necessitating a broad differential diagnosis and obtaining cultures from appropriate sites such as blood and urine. Early hospital admission is recommended for close monitoring, even in the absence of classic typhoid features. Empiric therapy should address enteric fever while also considering the child’s specific vulnerabilities. For instance, febrile neutropenic children with low absolute neutrophil counts (ANC) require broad gram-negative coverage as per oncology protocols, with therapy narrowed once Salmonella is confirmed and susceptibility results are available. In malnourished children, the risk of hypoglycemia and electrolyte imbalances is heightened, necessitating the integration of nutrition rehabilitation, glucose monitoring, and careful fluid management into the treatment plan. Initial stabilization often requires parenteral therapy, typically with intravenous ceftriaxone or cefotaxime, and the switch to oral therapy should be delayed until oral intake and absorption are reliable. Treatment duration may need to extend toward 14 days in cases of prolonged bacteremia, delayed fever clearance, or incomplete immune recovery. Drug toxicity and interactions, particularly with antiretrovirals or chemotherapy, should be closely monitored. Follow-up care is crucial, as relapse and complications may be harder to detect early in this population. Families should be counseled on strict food and water precautions to prevent reinfection, which can have devastating consequences for immunocompromised children. Licensed vaccines, schedules, efficacy and waning, contraindications, and catch-up or special group recommendations are not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ENTERIC FEVER MANAGEMENT IN CHILDREN WITH HIV
subtopics.category: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC MODIFICATIONS IN HIGH-RISK PEDIATRIC GROUPS
subtopics.concept: Enteric fever management in children living with HIV requires careful consideration due to their increased susceptibility to invasive salmonellosis, including non-typhoidal bacteremia, particularly in the context of advanced immunosuppression. These children may present with prolonged fever from multiple causes, necessitating a broader differential diagnosis and a lower threshold for microbiologic confirmation. Blood cultures should be obtained before initiating antibiotics whenever feasible, as microbiologic confirmation is critical in guiding therapy. Rapid typhoid tests should be interpreted cautiously due to potential limitations in sensitivity and specificity. Admission is recommended for children with advanced disease, characterized by low CD4 counts, lack of antiretroviral therapy (ART), severe malnutrition, or concurrent opportunistic infections, even if their initial vital signs appear stable. This is because such children are at higher risk for bacteremia and rapid clinical deterioration. For hospitalized patients, parenteral antibiotics should be used if oral absorption is uncertain, and early reassessment is essential to identify focal infections, such as bone pain, meningitic signs, or pneumonia, which may necessitate prolonged treatment. Antibiotic selection should prioritize agents with predictable pharmacokinetics, and therapy should be tailored based on susceptibility testing. In cases of documented bacteremia or slow clinical response, a longer duration of antibiotic therapy may be required compared to immunocompetent hosts. Close follow-up after discharge is essential to monitor for relapse. Drug–drug interactions must be actively checked, particularly when prescribing macrolides such as azithromycin, as these can increase QT risk and may interact with certain ART regimens. Reviewing the current ART combination and monitoring for interactions during co-administration is crucial. Preventive measures are highly beneficial. The typhoid conjugate vaccine (TCV), which is not a live vaccine, can be administered to children with HIV once acute illness has resolved, although the immune response may be reduced. Coordination with the treating team is necessary to optimize timing, and household WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) measures should be reinforced to minimize recurrent exposure.

case_studies.title: Enteric Fever in a Child with HIV: Admission Thresholds and Complications
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural area in India presents to the outpatient clinic with a 12-day history of intermittent high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and loose stools. His mother reports poor appetite and weight loss over the past month. He was diagnosed with HIV two years ago but has not been consistent with his antiretroviral therapy due to financial constraints. On examination, he appears lethargic, with a temperature of 39.2°C, mild pallor, and tender hepatosplenomegaly. His weight-for-age is below the 3rd percentile, and he has signs of moderate dehydration. No focal infections are noted on initial examination. A rapid typhoid test performed at a local clinic was positive, but no blood cultures were obtained. His mother mentions that he previously had pneumonia requiring hospitalization six months ago. The child’s vital signs are stable, but given his clinical history and poor nutritional status, the treating physician is concerned about the risk of bacteremia and potential complications.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand triage and admission thresholds for enteric fever in children with HIV, emphasizing the need for microbiologic confirmation and inpatient care in cases of advanced immunosuppression or malnutrition.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MANAGEMENT IN CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE AND RENAL IMPAIRMENT
subtopics.category: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC MODIFICATIONS IN HIGH-RISK PEDIATRIC GROUPS
subtopics.concept: Children with chronic kidney disease (CKD) are at heightened risk of rapid clinical deterioration during enteric fever due to the combined effects of dehydration, sepsis, and nephrotoxic co-medications, which can precipitate acute kidney injury (AKI) superimposed on CKD. Clinical evaluation in these patients must prioritize fluid balance and recent urine output, as vomiting and poor oral intake can quickly lead to fluid overload in this vulnerable group. Hospital admission should be strongly considered for children presenting with moderate dehydration, hypotension, altered sensorium, significant vomiting, or inability to maintain oral intake. Even in cases of mild illness, admission is advisable if home monitoring is unreliable or renal function is unstable. In hospitalized patients, strict intake-output monitoring and daily weight measurements should be documented whenever feasible, alongside routine monitoring of creatinine and electrolytes to guide fluid management and detect early signs of worsening renal function. Antimicrobial therapy in this population requires careful adjustment for renal impairment. Antibiotic selection should be guided by local susceptibility patterns, with doses adjusted according to pediatric renal dosing references, particularly for drugs with predominant renal clearance or when prolonged courses are necessary. Concurrent medications should be reviewed to avoid additive nephrotoxicity and to minimize risks of potassium imbalance or acid–base disturbances. For children on dialysis, coordination with nephrology is essential to appropriately time intravenous antibiotic doses around dialysis sessions, and catheter-related bloodstream infections should remain a key consideration in the differential diagnosis. Persistent fever with worsening renal indices warrants a broad diagnostic evaluation, including consideration of urinary tract infection, pneumonia, endocarditis, or focal abscess. Repeat cultures should be obtained rather than reflexively broadening antibiotic coverage. In managing enteric fever in children with CKD, clinicians must maintain a high index of suspicion for complications and adopt a cautious, multidisciplinary approach to optimize outcomes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ENTERIC FEVER IN SICKLE CELL DISEASE AND HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES
subtopics.category: DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC MODIFICATIONS IN HIGH-RISK PEDIATRIC GROUPS
subtopics.concept: Children with sickle cell disease (SCD) and other hemoglobinopathies are at heightened risk for invasive salmonellosis and metastatic infections, including osteomyelitis and deep abscesses. In this population, fever and bone pain can signify either a vaso-occlusive crisis with systemic inflammation or bacterial seeding, and timely differentiation is critical to prevent complications such as chronic osteomyelitis. A conservative approach to disposition is advised; febrile children with SCD should be admitted if enteric fever is suspected or if significant pain limits mobility. This is due to their increased susceptibility to bacteremia, anemia decompensation, and rapid clinical deterioration compared to immunocompetent peers. Blood cultures should be obtained prior to initiating antibiotics whenever feasible, and daily reassessment for focal signs such as localized tenderness, swelling, or reduced range of motion is essential to identify potential osteomyelitis or septic arthritis. Imaging plays a crucial role in early diagnosis. Ultrasound is useful for detecting abdominal collections, but cross-sectional imaging, particularly MRI, is preferred for evaluating suspected bone and joint involvement. Persisting focal pain or fever beyond the expected defervescence window warrants targeted imaging and, when feasible, aspiration or drainage for culture to refine therapy, especially in the context of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Antibiotic regimens may need to be extended beyond standard durations for uncomplicated typhoid fever when bacteremia or focal infections are confirmed. Multidisciplinary care involving hematology and orthopedics is strongly recommended to optimize outcomes. At discharge, caregivers should be provided with clear return precautions, emphasizing the need to seek immediate medical attention for recurrent fever or worsening focal pain. Reliable follow-up is essential to monitor for relapse or early signs of chronic bone infection, which can significantly impact long-term health outcomes.

case_studies.title: Enteric Fever in a Child with Sickle Cell Disease
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural area in central India, known to have sickle cell disease, presents to the emergency department with a 7-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and generalized weakness. His mother reports that he has been increasingly irritable, refusing to eat, and complaining of pain in his right thigh for the past 3 days. On examination, he is febrile (39.5°C), tachycardic, and appears pale. There is tenderness over the right femur without swelling or erythema, and his mobility is significantly reduced due to pain. Abdominal examination reveals mild hepatomegaly but no guarding or rebound tenderness. Laboratory investigations reveal hemoglobin of 7.4 g/dL and leukocytosis. Blood cultures have been sent. The family is concerned as his symptoms have not improved despite being seen at a local clinic and starting oral antibiotics 4 days ago. They report no recent travel or known typhoid vaccination.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of differentiating vaso-occlusive crisis from invasive bacterial infections like enteric fever in children with sickle cell disease, and understand the need for prompt admission and further evaluation in cases with persistent feve

subtopics.subtopic_title: GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING RESUSCITATION AND TRANSFUSION THRESHOLDS
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Gastrointestinal bleeding in enteric fever typically occurs during the second to third week of illness due to ulceration of Peyer’s patches and may range from occult blood loss to massive hemorrhage. Clinical suspicion should arise with signs such as melena, hematochezia, hematemesis, tachycardia disproportionate to fever, pallor, orthostatic symptoms, or a falling hemoglobin level. Initial management focuses on resuscitation and stabilization, which includes assessing airway and perfusion, establishing intravenous access, administering isotonic fluids, and early blood crossmatching. Packed red blood cell transfusion is indicated in the presence of hemodynamic instability, ongoing brisk bleeding, or significant anemia. For stable children, a hemoglobin threshold of ≤7 g/dL is a pragmatic guide for transfusion, but higher thresholds should be used in cases of shock, hypoxemia, or significant comorbidities. Coagulopathy should be monitored by checking prothrombin time/INR and platelet counts, and clinically significant abnormalities should be treated with plasma or platelets. Effective anti-typhoid antibiotic therapy must be continued, as bacterial eradication is essential for cessation of bleeding. In Indian settings, it is important to evaluate for alternative or concurrent causes of bleeding, such as dengue (characterized by thrombocytopenia and capillary leak) or peptic ulcer disease. Severe or persistent bleeding warrants early consultation with gastroenterology and surgical teams. While endoscopy is not routinely required, it may be lifesaving if a focal bleeding source is suspected and appropriate facilities are available. Admission to a high-dependency unit or intensive care unit is recommended for children requiring transfusion, those with unstable vital signs, or those exhibiting changes in mental status. Families should be counseled to seek prompt medical attention if black stools recur during convalescence, as rebleeding can occur.

case_studies.title: Gastrointestinal Bleeding in a Child with Enteric Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of high-grade fever and abdominal pain. His parents report dark, tarry stools for the past two days and increasing lethargy. On examination, the child appears pale and drowsy, with a heart rate of 140 bpm, blood pressure of 84/50 mmHg, and capillary refill time of 4 seconds. His temperature is 38.5°C. Abdominal examination reveals mild distension and generalized tenderness without guarding. Laboratory results show a hemoglobin level of 6.5 g/dL, and stool occult blood is positive. The family reports no history of NSAID use, and there is no known history of dengue fever in the area. The facility does not have immediate access to endoscopy services.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize gastrointestinal bleeding in enteric fever, assess resuscitation needs, and determine transfusion thresholds based on clinical and laboratory findings.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RECOGNITION AND EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT OF INTESTINAL PERFORATION
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Intestinal perforation is the most serious complication of typhoid fever and constitutes a surgical emergency. It typically occurs 2–3 weeks into untreated or inadequately treated illness, though it may present earlier in severe cases. Clinicians should suspect intestinal perforation in a child with prolonged fever who develops sudden worsening abdominal pain, progressive distension, guarding or rigidity, persistent vomiting, shock, or an abrupt change in sensorium. In younger children, the presentation can be subtle, necessitating serial abdominal examinations in all admitted cases. Immediate management focuses on resuscitation and urgent surgical evaluation. Establish intravenous (IV) access promptly, treat shock with crystalloid boluses, and initiate early vasoactive support if the shock is fluid-refractory. Oxygen should be provided, and the child kept nil per os. Blood cultures, if not previously obtained, should be collected, and baseline investigations including hemoglobin, electrolytes, lactate (where available), and coagulation studies should be repeated. Empiric broad-spectrum parenteral antibiotics targeting typhoidal Salmonella and gut flora must be started, guided by local antimicrobial protocols. Ceftriaxone combined with metronidazole is commonly used, with escalation to carbapenems if extensively drug-resistant (XDR) infection is suspected or confirmed. Confirmatory imaging should not delay surgery in unstable children. An upright abdominal radiograph may reveal free air, while ultrasonography can identify free fluid and bowel wall changes when radiographic findings are inconclusive. Early surgical intervention, whether primary repair or resection as determined by the surgical team, significantly improves outcomes compared to delayed management. Post-operatively, effective antibiotic therapy should be continued, and the child should be closely monitored for sepsis, electrolyte imbalances, and other complications. High-dependency or intensive care unit (ICU) management is essential for children requiring vasoactive support or mechanical ventilation. Timely recognition and prompt multidisciplinary intervention are critical to improving survival and reducing morbidity in paediatric intestinal perforation secondary to typhoid fever.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SEPSIS BUNDLE MANAGEMENT OF SHOCK AND ENCEPHALOPATHY
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Complicated enteric fever in children can present with septic shock, encephalopathy, and multi-organ dysfunction, necessitating prompt management using standard paediatric sepsis bundles. Initial steps include providing oxygen and airway support in cases of respiratory distress or low oxygen saturation, establishing intravenous access, and rapidly assessing perfusion, mental status, and blood glucose levels. Fluid resuscitation should begin with 20 mL/kg boluses of isotonic crystalloid, with frequent reassessment to monitor for fluid overload; some children may require up to 40–60 mL/kg. For fluid-refractory shock, vasopressors should be initiated early in accordance with local paediatric shock protocols, and patients should be managed in a high-dependency unit or intensive care unit. Antibiotics are integral to resuscitation, with blood cultures obtained prior to administration if this does not delay care. Effective parenteral antibiotics should be started within the first hour, with intravenous ceftriaxone being appropriate in most settings. In cases of suspected extensively drug-resistant (XDR) infections or failure on cephalosporins, escalation to meropenem is recommended. Critically ill children may benefit from combination therapy with meropenem and azithromycin while awaiting susceptibility results. Hypoglycemia and electrolyte disturbances must be corrected promptly, and organ support should be provided as needed. Monitoring for abdominal complications, such as ileus or perforation, is essential, with urinary catheter placement to track urine output and lactate measurement where available. Clinically significant bleeding should be treated with blood products, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) should be considered in refractory shock. Adjunctive corticosteroids are not routinely indicated but may be considered in critically ill children with severe typhoid presenting with shock or obtundation. High-dose dexamethasone has historical evidence of benefit in such cases but should only be used after excluding perforation and with specialist input. Intensive monitoring should continue until hemodynamic stability and mental status improve, after which cautious step-down care can be planned, ensuring completion of an adequate antibiotic course.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PROTOCOLIZED DEXAMETHASONE FOR SEVERE TYPHOID WITH SHOCK OR OBTUNDATION
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Protocolized dexamethasone therapy is not a routine treatment for enteric fever but may be life-saving in children with severe typhoid presenting with “typhoid encephalopathy” and shock. High-dose dexamethasone should only be considered in critically ill paediatric patients exhibiting obtundation, seizures, or coma alongside hypotensive shock attributable to enteric fever. Before initiating corticosteroids, it is essential to rapidly exclude intestinal perforation or other surgical abdominal conditions. Dexamethasone must be administered in parallel with appropriate antibiotics and comprehensive sepsis management, as it is not a substitute for escalation of antimicrobial therapy. A practical regimen, supported by severe typhoid studies and some paediatric guidelines, involves an initial intravenous loading dose of dexamethasone at 3 mg/kg, followed by 1 mg/kg every six hours for 48 hours. This treatment should be delivered in a monitored setting, preferably in a paediatric intensive care unit (PICU/ICU), with frequent neurological and haemodynamic assessments. Close monitoring for potential adverse effects, including hyperglycaemia, gastrointestinal bleeding, secondary infections, and masking of intestinal perforation symptoms, is critical. A low threshold should be maintained for repeat abdominal examinations and imaging if the child develops worsening pain or distension. The planned course of dexamethasone should be completed without tapering, given the short treatment duration, and the diagnostic label should be reassessed if there is no rapid improvement in shock or sensorium. Corticosteroids should not be used in uncomplicated typhoid, isolated persistent fever, or in cases with uncontrolled gastrointestinal bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia with bleeding risk, or a strong suspicion of untreated alternative infections such as malaria or dengue. This approach underscores the importance of careful patient selection and vigilant monitoring to optimise outcomes in severe typhoid with shock or obtundation.

case_studies.title: Severe Typhoid with Shock and Altered Sensorium
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old girl from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and progressive lethargy. Over the past 12 hours, her family noted she became increasingly drowsy and developed cold extremities. On examination, she is obtunded with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 9/15, febrile at 39.5°C, with a heart rate of 140 bpm, blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg, and delayed capillary refill of 4 seconds. Her abdomen is diffusely tender without guarding or rebound, and bowel sounds are diminished. No signs of overt gastrointestinal bleeding are observed. Laboratory results show a leukocyte count of 3,500/mm³ and platelet count of 90,000/mm³. A rapid malaria test is negative. Blood cultures are pending. Despite a fluid bolus, her hypotension persists, and she remains unresponsive. The team is concerned about severe typhoid with possible encephalopathy and shock.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RESPIRATORY COMPLICATIONS INCLUDING PNEUMONIA AND ARDS
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Respiratory complications, including pneumonia and acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), are recognized but uncommon in paediatric typhoid and enteric fever. Early respiratory symptoms, such as cough, mild tachypnoea, and fever, may occur in uncomplicated cases, but the presence of hypoxia or focal chest signs warrants prompt evaluation for complications or alternative diagnoses. Severe pneumonia and ARDS are more likely in children with delayed therapy, sepsis, or co-infections. Early complications include persistent tachypnoea, chest indrawing, oxygen saturation below age-appropriate norms, and increasing work of breathing, which should prompt escalation of care. Late complications, such as pleural effusion or empyema, require daily reassessment as ARDS can progress rapidly. Red-flag warning signs include persistent hypoxia, worsening respiratory distress, and failure to respond to initial therapy. Differential diagnoses that mimic typhoid with respiratory distress, such as bacterial pneumonia, malaria with acidosis, dengue with plasma leak, and multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C), should be carefully considered to avoid diagnostic anchoring based on prior serology results. The pathophysiology of respiratory complications in typhoid fever is not specified in the source. However, it is emphasized that these complications are often associated with systemic inflammation, delayed treatment, or secondary infections. Bedside monitoring should include pulse oximetry, targeted chest imaging, and blood gas analysis when clinically indicated. Children with signs of respiratory failure should be managed in a monitored setting, such as an intensive care unit (ICU) or paediatric ICU (PICU), to facilitate rapid escalation of care. Definitive management follows paediatric sepsis and respiratory failure protocols. Supplemental oxygen should be provided, and shock must be treated promptly, avoiding excessive intravenous fluids in cases of evolving lung injury. Non-invasive support, such as continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP), or invasive ventilation should be initiated if oxygenation fails. Effective anti-typhoidal antibiotics must be continued, with additional pneumonia coverage added only if imaging, auscultation, or biomarkers suggest a secondary bacterial process. Critical care involvement is crucial in cases of ARDS due to the potential for rapid deterioration. Follow-up involves daily reassessment for complications like pleural effusion or empyema.

case_studies.title: Respiratory Complications in Paediatric Typhoid Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to a district hospital with a 10-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and intermittent dry cough. Over the past 24 hours, his parents report worsening breathing difficulty and lethargy. On examination, he is febrile (39.5°C), tachypnoeic (RR 38/min), and has oxygen saturation of 88% on room air. Chest auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds in the right lower lung zone with coarse crackles. He has moderate subcostal and intercostal retractions. His pulse rate is 120/min, and he appears dehydrated with delayed capillary refill. A bedside chest X-ray shows consolidation in the right lower lobe. A rapid malaria test is negative. Blood cultures and additional investigations are pending. Despite initial oxygen supplementation via nasal cannula, his respiratory distress worsens, requiring escalation to high-flow nasal oxygen. The admitting physician considers ICU transfer due to persistent hypoxia and signs of impending respiratory failure.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and triage respiratory complications such as pneumonia and ARDS in paediatric typhoid fever, including red-flag signs requiring urgent escalation of care.

Fever is expected in typhoid fever and does not alone necessitate ICU transfer.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RENAL AND ELECTROLYTE COMPLICATIONS INCLUDING ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Renal and electrolyte complications in paediatric typhoid and enteric fever are primarily secondary to dehydration and sepsis, with early findings often nonspecific and easily overlooked. Early complications include pre-renal azotemia caused by poor oral intake and diarrhea, while late complications may involve intrinsic acute kidney injury (AKI) during shock and multi-organ dysfunction. Rare immune-mediated phenomena, such as acute nephritis and glomerulonephritis, should be considered in cases presenting with hematuria, hypertension, or proteinuria. Red-flag warning signs requiring urgent attention include persistent oliguria despite adequate resuscitation, rising creatinine levels, refractory acidosis, hyperkalemia, or pulmonary edema. Additionally, unexplained hematuria accompanied by systemic toxicity warrants nephrology consultation. The pathophysiology of renal involvement in enteric fever is linked to hypoperfusion due to dehydration and septic shock, which can lead to pre-renal azotemia or intrinsic renal damage. Immune-mediated renal injury, though uncommon, may result from inflammatory processes triggered by the infection. Bedside monitoring should focus on urine output, hydration status, and baseline creatinine and electrolyte levels, particularly in children presenting with vomiting, diarrhea, poor oral intake, or hypotension. Frequent reassessment of hydration and perfusion is crucial to avoid both under-resuscitation and fluid overload, especially in septic patients. Hypoglycemia and electrolyte abnormalities, such as hyponatremia, must be corrected promptly to prevent encephalopathy and seizures. Definitive management includes tailored antibiotic therapy and supportive care, with adjustments based on renal function. Nephrotoxic medications should be avoided when possible, and renal dosing references must be used for drugs requiring adjustment, particularly in children undergoing prolonged treatment or those with pre-existing renal disease. Escalation of care is necessary in cases of persistent oliguria, worsening renal function, or severe complications like refractory acidosis or pulmonary edema. Nephrology input is essential for suspected intrinsic nephritis, the need for renal replacement therapy, or systemic toxicity with unexplained hematuria. Follow-up care should address ongoing renal monitoring and management of any residual renal dysfunction.

case_studies.title: Typhoid Fever with Renal Complications in a Dehydrated Child
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with a 7-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and non-bloody diarrhea. His parents report poor oral intake and lethargy over the last 3 days. On examination, he appears dehydrated with dry mucous membranes, a delayed capillary refill time of 4 seconds, and a weak peripheral pulse. His blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg, and his heart rate is 140 beats per minute. Urine output has been minimal for the past 12 hours. A focused bedside assessment reveals diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding or rigidity. Initial laboratory investigations show a serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL (elevated) and mild hyponatremia (Na 129 mmol/L). The child remains febrile despite antipyretics and fluid boluses. His parents are concerned about worsening fatigue and drowsiness.

case_studies.title: Typhoid Fever with Acute Kidney Injury in a Dehydrated Child
case_studies.vignette: A 7-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with a 10-day history of high-grade fever, abdominal pain, and loose stools. His parents report poor oral intake for the past 3 days and a decrease in urine output over the last 24 hours. On examination, he is lethargic, febrile (39.5°C), tachycardic (HR 130/min), and hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg). His mucous membranes are dry, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. Abdominal examination reveals diffuse tenderness without guarding. Initial bedside investigations show a urine output of 0.4 mL/kg/hour and a capillary blood glucose of 68 mg/dL. Point-of-care serum creatinine is elevated at 1.8 mg/dL (age-appropriate baseline ~0.5 mg/dL).
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize renal and electrolyte complications, including acute kidney injury, in a child with typhoid fever presenting with dehydration and sepsis, and identify red-flag signs requiring urgent intervention.

subtopics.subtopic_title: COAGULOPATHY AND DISSEMINATED INTRAVASCULAR COAGULATION MANAGEMENT
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Coagulopathy and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in paediatric enteric fever are systemic complications that require prompt recognition and management. Clinically significant bleeding in enteric fever is most commonly due to intestinal ulceration but may also result from consumptive coagulopathy and DIC in severe systemic disease. DIC should be suspected in a septic child presenting with diffuse oozing from venipuncture sites, mucosal bleeding, purpura with shock, or rapid laboratory deterioration. Initial evaluation must include a repeat complete blood count (CBC) with platelet count, coagulation profile (PT, APTT, and fibrinogen when available), and markers of organ dysfunction. Interpretation of results should be contextualized clinically, as mild thrombocytopenia is common and does not necessarily predict major bleeding. Concurrent drivers of bleeding, such as hepatic dysfunction, vitamin deficiency, or co-infection with dengue, should be actively sought. Management priorities include treating the underlying infection and shock while supporting hemostasis. Blood products should be reserved for active bleeding or high-risk procedures rather than isolated laboratory abnormalities. Platelet transfusion is indicated for clinically significant bleeding with thrombocytopenia or critically low platelet counts even in the absence of bleeding, provided the child is in a monitored setting. Prolonged coagulation should be corrected with fresh frozen plasma (FFP) or cryoprecipitate in cases of bleeding with documented factor deficiency or low fibrinogen levels. Vitamin K should be administered when deficiency is plausible, such as in cases of prolonged poor intake, cholestasis, or extended antibiotic use. Medications that impair platelet function must be avoided. Escalation to intensive care is warranted for bleeding associated with shock, altered sensorium, or rapidly progressive organ dysfunction. Early involvement of haematology is essential in cases of severe or refractory DIC. These steps are critical for optimizing outcomes in paediatric patients with systemic complications of enteric fever.

case_studies.title: Coagulopathy and DIC in Enteric Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with persistent high-grade fever for 10 days, abdominal pain, and progressive lethargy. His parents report dark-colored stools and bleeding from his gums over the past 24 hours. He appears pale and drowsy, with a heart rate of 140 bpm, blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg, and cool extremities. On examination, he has petechiae over his lower limbs, diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding, and oozing from venipuncture sites. His initial laboratory results show hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, platelet count of 45,000/µL, and prolonged prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT). A repeat CBC confirms worsening thrombocytopenia. The child is clinically unstable and requires urgent intervention in a monitored setting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: FOCAL SUPPURATIVE COMPLICATIONS INCLUDING OSTEOMYELITIS AND ABSCESS
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Focal suppurative complications, including osteomyelitis and abscess formation, are rare but significant sequelae of typhoid and enteric fever in children, arising from bacteremia that seeds localized sites. These complications typically present with persistent fever beyond the expected recovery period and localized symptoms such as bone pain, limp, joint swelling, focal abdominal tenderness, or new neurologic deficits. Early complications include bone and joint infections, such as osteomyelitis or septic arthritis, and deep soft-tissue or hepatosplenic abscesses. Late complications are not specified in the source. Children with hemoglobinopathies, chronic granulomatous disease, or other immunodeficiencies are at heightened risk, although previously healthy children can also develop focal disease. Red-flag warning signs include persistent fever despite clinical stabilization and the aforementioned localized symptoms, which warrant further investigation. The pathophysiology involves Salmonella bacteremia leading to seeding of focal sites, particularly in immunocompromised or predisposed children. Bedside monitoring should focus on tracking fever resolution, localized symptoms, and functional recovery, such as the return of weight-bearing ability. Persistent symptoms or failure to improve should prompt reassessment for antimicrobial resistance, inadequate source control, or alternative diagnoses. Escalation of care is necessary if there is clinical deterioration or suspicion of abscess, empyema, or septic arthritis, requiring multidisciplinary coordination. Definitive management involves prolonged antibiotic therapy tailored to microbiological findings, as focal suppurative infections demand longer treatment durations than uncomplicated typhoid fever. Cultures from blood and any drained fluid or aspirate are essential to guide therapy, especially given the variability in antimicrobial resistance patterns. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics should be avoided without microbiological confirmation to minimize antimicrobial resistance pressure. Imaging plays a critical role in diagnosis, with ultrasound recommended for suspected abdominal collections and MRI or CT for bone, deep tissue, or organ abscesses when initial studies are inconclusive. Early involvement of surgical, orthopedic, or interventional radiology teams is crucial for drainage and source control. Clinical improvement, including symptom resolution and functional recovery, should guide the duration of therapy, with close follow-up to monitor for relapse or complications.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RECOGNITION AND SUPPORTIVE MANAGEMENT OF TYPHOID MYOCARDITIS
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Typhoid myocarditis is an uncommon but significant complication of enteric fever, necessitating prompt recognition and supportive management in paediatric patients. Myocardial involvement should be suspected when tachycardia is disproportionate to fever, or when clinical findings such as a new gallop rhythm, arrhythmias, chest pain, syncope, signs of heart failure (including hepatomegaly and pulmonary edema), or persistent shock despite adequate volume resuscitation are present. Cardiac dysfunction may manifest either as part of severe sepsis or as a focal complication. Initial bedside evaluation should include a thorough cardiovascular examination, electrocardiography (ECG) to detect conduction abnormalities and arrhythmias, and echocardiography when available to assess ventricular function and rule out pericardial effusion. Cardiac biomarkers may aid diagnosis but should not delay stabilization. Management is primarily supportive and should be conducted in a high-dependency unit or intensive care unit (ICU) if the child presents with shock, arrhythmia, or respiratory compromise. Excessive fluid administration should be avoided once myocardial dysfunction is suspected, and vasoactive support and inotropes should be used, guided by echocardiographic findings and bedside perfusion assessments. Ventilatory support is indicated in cases of pulmonary edema or fatigue. Treating the underlying infection with an appropriate anti-typhoid antibiotic regimen, such as intravenous ceftriaxone, is crucial, with escalation to alternative agents if extensively drug-resistant (XDR) typhoid is suspected. Improvement in myocardial function often parallels the resolution of bacteremia. The use of immunomodulators, including corticosteroids or intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), is not well-supported by evidence and should only be considered in cases of refractory cardiogenic shock under specialist guidance. Cardiology follow-up is essential after recovery, as most children regain normal cardiac function. However, persistent dysfunction requires repeat echocardiography and tailored activity recommendations. Early recognition and targeted supportive care are critical to improving outcomes in paediatric typhoid myocarditis.

case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the emergency department with persistent high-grade fever for 10 days, abdominal pain, and lethargy. His parents report worsening fatigue and difficulty breathing over the past 24 hours. On examination, he is febrile (39.5°C), tachycardic (heart rate 160 bpm), and hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg), with cool extremities and delayed capillary refill. Auscultation reveals a new gallop rhythm and mild bilateral basal crackles. Hepatomegaly is noted on abdominal palpation. Despite initial fluid boluses, his perfusion remains poor, and he develops worsening respiratory distress. Bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST changes. Chest X-ray reveals mild pulmonary edema. The team suspects a cardiac complication and prepares for further evaluation and supportive care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: EVALUATION AND SUPPORTIVE CARE FOR TYPHOID HEPATITIS AND CHOLECYSTITIS
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Mild hepatic involvement is a common feature of enteric fever in children, often presenting as modest elevations in transaminases during the bacteremic phase. However, clinically apparent jaundice is less frequent and warrants evaluation for alternative or concurrent diagnoses prevalent in regions like India, such as acute viral hepatitis, malaria, leptospirosis, and dengue. The term "typhoid hepatitis" should be considered when a child with suspected enteric fever develops jaundice accompanied by tender hepatomegaly and elevated transaminases that do not reach the levels typical of fulminant viral hepatitis. Management of typhoid hepatitis is primarily supportive while continuing effective anti-typhoid antibiotics. It is essential to review all medications and avoid hepatotoxic agents where possible. Close monitoring of hydration, glucose levels, and coagulation parameters, including prothrombin time/INR, is critical, as significant hepatic dysfunction can lead to hypoglycemia and coagulopathy. Prolonged INR should be managed with vitamin K, and plasma or other blood products may be required in cases of clinically significant bleeding or before impending procedures. Escalation to high-dependency or intensive care unit (ICU) settings is necessary if encephalopathy, progressive coagulopathy, or shock develops. Right upper quadrant pain, persistent vomiting, or ongoing fever despite appropriate antibiotics should raise suspicion for hepatobiliary complications such as acalculous cholecystitis or cholangitis. Abdominal ultrasonography is the preferred diagnostic tool to identify gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or biliary dilatation. Most hepatobiliary complications resolve with antibiotics and supportive care, and surgical intervention is rarely required. However, surgery is indicated if gallbladder perforation or uncontrolled sepsis is suspected. Liver enzymes should be re-evaluated during follow-up to confirm a downward trend after the resolution of fever.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RECOGNITION OF HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS TRIGGERED BY ENTERIC FEVER
subtopics.category: RECOGNITION AND ACUTE MANAGEMENT OF SYSTEMIC COMPLICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a rare but potentially fatal hyperinflammatory syndrome that can be triggered by severe infections, including enteric fever. In paediatric patients, HLH should be suspected when a child diagnosed or suspected to have enteric fever deteriorates or fails to improve despite appropriate antibiotic therapy. Key clinical features suggestive of HLH include persistent high fever, hepatosplenomegaly, worsening cytopenias, liver dysfunction, coagulopathy, and markedly elevated ferritin levels. Early recognition is crucial, and evaluation should be initiated urgently with specialist input. Investigations should include a repeat complete blood count (CBC), liver enzymes, triglycerides, fibrinogen, ferritin, and coagulation studies, alongside a review of cultures to confirm the adequacy of the anti-typhoid regimen. Although bone marrow examination may reveal hemophagocytosis, it is not essential for initial stabilization and should not delay treatment in critically ill children. Clinicians should remain vigilant for overlapping features of sepsis and HLH, ensuring reassessment for uncontrolled infection, intestinal perforation, or other occult foci while simultaneously pursuing a hyperinflammation workup. Management of HLH in this context requires addressing both the underlying infection and the dysregulated immune response. Effective antibiotics should be continued, with escalation if resistance is suspected. Immunomodulatory therapy, typically high-dose corticosteroids and occasionally intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), should be initiated under the guidance of hematology and intensive care teams. In severe cases, protocol-based therapy may be necessary. Supportive care in a high-dependency unit or intensive care unit (ICU) is essential to manage complications such as shock, bleeding, and organ dysfunction. Prompt identification and treatment of HLH triggered by enteric fever significantly improve outcomes by controlling hyperinflammation while eradicating the underlying infection. This comprehensive approach underscores the importance of early recognition and multidisciplinary management in paediatric cases.

case_studies.title: Recognition of Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis Triggered by Enteric Fever
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy from a rural setting in India presents to the emergency department with persistent high-grade fever for 14 days, despite completing a 7-day course of oral cefixime for suspected typhoid fever. His parents report worsening fatigue, poor appetite, and abdominal distension over the past week. On examination, he appears pale and lethargic, with a temperature of 39.5°C. There is marked hepatosplenomegaly, mild icterus, and petechial rash on the lower limbs. Focused labs reveal pancytopenia: hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, platelet count 45,000/μL, and white blood cell count 2,800/μL. Liver enzymes are elevated (AST 320 IU/L, ALT 280 IU/L), and serum ferritin is markedly raised at 6,000 ng/mL. Parents deny recent travel or other significant exposures. Blood cultures are pending, and the child’s condition is deteriorating with hypotension and tachycardia. The treating physician suspects a hyperinflammatory syndrome complicating enteric fever and considers urgent escalation of care.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) in a child with suspected or confirmed enteric fever who deteriorates or fails to improve despite appropriate antibiotic therapy.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RELAPSE EVALUATION AND POST-TREATMENT FOLLOW-UP PLAN
subtopics.category: RELAPSE FOLLOW-UP AND CARRIER STATE MANAGEMENT
subtopics.concept: Relapse evaluation and post-treatment follow-up are crucial in managing typhoid and enteric fever in paediatric patients, as relapse and late complications may occur even after apparent recovery. Clinical review should be scheduled 7–10 days after completing antibiotics to confirm weight gain, resolution of abdominal symptoms, and absence of recurrent fever. Families should be counseled that relapse occurs in approximately 5–10% of children, typically within 1–3 weeks post-therapy, particularly if the illness was prolonged before treatment or if antibiotic courses were shortened. If fever recurs following a documented or probable episode of enteric fever, it should be treated as a relapse until proven otherwise. A repeat blood culture should be obtained before restarting antibiotics whenever feasible, and focal complications such as intra-abdominal abscesses should be evaluated as potential bacterial reservoirs. In cases where the child appears toxic, therapy should be initiated promptly without waiting for culture results, with a switch to an antibiotic class different from the initial regimen. For instance, cefixime may be replaced with azithromycin or oral therapy may be escalated to intravenous ceftriaxone depending on severity. Once susceptibility results are available, treatment should be tailored accordingly and completed over an adequate duration, often 10–14 days in relapse cases, with strict adherence emphasized. Children may generally return to school once afebrile and clinically well for at least 48 hours on effective antibiotics, provided reliable hand hygiene practices are maintained. Families should also be counseled on WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) practices to prevent reinfection, particularly if the source of infection remains. The follow-up visit should include a review of household risk factors, discussion of catch-up typhoid conjugate vaccine (TCV) if not already administered, and clear guidance on when to seek urgent care for symptoms such as new abdominal pain, bleeding, or altered mental status. This comprehensive approach ensures early identification of relapse and reduces the risk of complications or reinfection.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DEFINITION AND DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH TO CARRIER STATES
subtopics.category: RELAPSE FOLLOW-UP AND CARRIER STATE MANAGEMENT
subtopics.concept: A carrier state in typhoid and enteric fever is defined as the continued shedding of *Salmonella* in stool, and less commonly in urine, following clinical recovery. Convalescent carriage refers to shedding up to 3 months after illness, temporary carriage to 3–12 months, and chronic carriage to shedding that persists beyond 12 months. Chronic carriers are rare in children but are significant in outbreak investigations and households with high transmission risk. Routine stool cultures are not recommended for all children recovering uneventfully from typhoid fever. Instead, targeted screening is advised for children who are part of a cluster of cases, experience repeated episodes of enteric fever, live with vulnerable contacts, or belong to households where food handling is a concern. To differentiate transient shedding from chronic carriage, stool cultures should ideally be collected at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics. Since shedding is intermittent, multiple stool specimens—typically three, spaced days to weeks apart—are necessary to confirm or exclude carriage. If carriage is identified, it is essential to evaluate for underlying conditions that may hinder eradication, such as gallbladder disease or biliary abnormalities. Abdominal ultrasonography may be useful to detect gallstones, biliary sludge, or chronic cholecystitis. Urine cultures should be considered if urinary symptoms are present or if schistosomiasis is a concern in endemic regions. Public health coordination is critical in managing carrier states. Children identified as carriers may require restrictions on school attendance and food handling, and household measures to improve water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) should be implemented. Reporting to public health authorities ensures appropriate guidance on these precautions and facilitates planning for eradication therapy.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ERADICATION STRATEGIES FOR CONFIRMED CHRONIC CARRIERS
subtopics.category: RELAPSE FOLLOW-UP AND CARRIER STATE MANAGEMENT
subtopics.concept: Eradication therapy is essential for confirmed chronic carriers of typhoid due to the risk of onward transmission and recurrent outbreaks. Antibiotic selection should be guided by susceptibility results and prior treatment history, with a prolonged course recommended to address persistent organisms in the gallbladder or biliary biofilm. Traditional regimens include 4–6 weeks of high-dose oral amoxicillin or co-trimoxazole for susceptible isolates. Fluoroquinolones, although historically effective in adults, are discouraged for routine use in children and should only be considered with specialist input when susceptibility is confirmed and other options are unsuitable. Close monitoring during therapy is necessary to ensure adherence and to detect potential adverse effects such as gastrointestinal intolerance or hematologic toxicity, particularly with co-trimoxazole. Treatment start and end dates must be documented, and coordination with public health authorities is required to enforce restrictions on food handling during therapy. Following treatment completion, clearance stool cultures are mandatory because clinical well-being does not confirm eradication. Since shedding is intermittent, multiple negative stool specimens are needed to confirm clearance. If gallbladder disease is present, antibiotic therapy alone may be insufficient for eradication. Surgical options, such as cholecystectomy, should be discussed in cases of symptomatic gallstones or chronic cholecystitis, especially when repeated eradication attempts fail. Combining cholecystectomy with antibiotics can improve cure rates in selected cases, but operative decisions must consider the child’s age, nutritional status, and the availability of local surgical expertise. Throughout eradication therapy and follow-up, household WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) practices should be reinforced to reduce exposure and prevent reinfection, which could be mistaken for persistent carriage. Not specified in the source are details regarding stool culture schedules, criteria for clearance, or specific household and school precautions beyond WASH practices. Public health reporting requirements are also not explicitly addressed in the source.
